questions.question: What percentage of individuals with severe mental illness also have substance use disorder?
questions.explanation: 50% of individuals with severe mental illness also have substance use disorder, and vice versa. This high co-occurrence demonstrates why integrated treatment addressing both simultaneously shows best outcomes. Treating only one condition while ignoring the other typically fails—mental health symptoms drive continued use, and substance use prevents mental health recovery.

questions.question: What is the difference between enabling and supporting a family member with substance use?

options.text: Enabling (maintains use): covering consequences, rescuing, making excuses; Supporting (promotes recovery): allowing consequences, boundaries, attending Al-Anon, self-care
Enabling is good; supporting is bad
Only professionals can support; families only enable

questions.explanation: Enabling maintains use by protecting from consequences: covering (calling in sick for them), rescuing from legal/financial problems, making excuses, doing their responsibilities, threats without follow-through. Supporting promotes recovery while protecting family: allowing natural consequences, maintaining boundaries, expressing concern without controlling, attending Al-Anon, taking care of yourself, following through on boundaries. Enabling protects from consequences; supporting allows consequences while offering path to recovery.

questions.question: For adolescent substance use, what type of therapy shows superior outcomes to individual therapy?
questions.explanation: Family therapy for adolescent substance use (Multidimensional Family Therapy/MDFT, Brief Strategic Family Therapy/BSFT, Functional Family Therapy/FFT) shows superior outcomes to individual therapy alone. This addresses family patterns maintaining use, improves communication, strengthens parent-teen relationship, and treats teen in their primary environment. Research demonstrates strong evidence for family-based approaches.

questions.question: What is the evidence grade for family involvement in substance use treatment?

options.text: Strong for adolescent family therapy; Moderate-Strong for family recovery programs (Al-Anon)

questions.explanation: Evidence grade is STRONG for family therapy effectiveness with adolescent substance use (superior to individual treatment). Evidence is MODERATE-to-STRONG for family recovery programs like Al-Anon/Nar-Anon improving family member wellbeing regardless of whether person using substances achieves sobriety. Family involvement significantly improves treatment outcomes across studies.

questions.question: When does substance use require emergency help?

options.text: Suspected overdose, suicidal thoughts + substance use, severe withdrawal, violent behavior while intoxicated, mixing substances

questions.explanation: Call 911 or go to ER immediately for: suspected overdose (unresponsive, shallow breathing, blue lips/nails), suicidal thoughts combined with substance use (dramatically increases risk), severe withdrawal symptoms (seizures, hallucinations, confusion), violent behavior while intoxicated, or mixing substances (especially opioids + benzodiazepines, alcohol + pills). Don't wait for certainty—substance-related emergencies can be fatal. Better safe than sorry.

title: Substance Use, Addiction, and Family Mental Health - Quiz
